coredata.dc:description: Development of suitable materials to address the problems posed by emerging infections such as coronavirus illness 2019 and the high prevalence of cancer as a significant cause of death worldwide is a crucial field of research. Globally, the scientific community is exploring novel materials and biological systems to combat a range of illnesses that impact both humans and animals. Carbon is one of the most crucial elements in the earthy civilization, and it is capable of forming stronger bonds than any other material in different forms. Biomedical researchers, nowadays are increasingly exploring carbon nanostructures owing to its ease of modification, unique structural characteristics, excellent optical activity, ability to carry drugs, biocompatibility, and effectiveness against viruses and bacteria. Nevertheless, the task of minimizing toxicity, improving biological compatibility, boosting dispersibility, and augmenting water solubility appears to be challenging for carbon-based biomedical systems. However, the functionalization of these carbon-based nanomaterials (CBNs) mitigates their toxicity and renders them soluble in a variety of solvents, including water, which is required for biomedical applications. Therefore, this article aimsto summarize the latest advances in the field of carbon-based nanomaterials, specifically nanodiamonds, fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene, graphene oxide, and graphene quantum dots, for drug delivery, cancer therapy, bio-sensing, antibacterials, antiviral and tissue engineering applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of suitable materials to address the problems posed by emerging infections such as coronavirus illness 2019 and the high prevalence of cancer as a significant cause of death worldwide is a crucial field of research. Globally, the scientific community is exploring novel materials and biological systems to combat a range of illnesses that impact both humans and animals. Carbon is one of the most crucial elements in the earthy civilization, and it is capable of forming stronger bonds than any other material in different forms. Biomedical researchers, nowadays are increasingly exploring carbon nanostructures owing to its ease of modification, unique structural characteristics, excellent optical activity, ability to carry drugs, biocompatibility, and effectiveness against viruses and bacteria. Nevertheless, the task of minimizing toxicity, improving biological compatibility, boosting dispersibility, and augmenting water solubility appears to be challenging for carbon-based biomedical systems. However, the functionalization of these carbon-based nanomaterials (CBNs) mitigates their toxicity and renders them soluble in a variety of solvents, including water, which is required for biomedical applications. Therefore, this article aimsto summarize the latest advances in the field of carbon-based nanomaterials, specifically nanodiamonds, fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene, graphene oxide, and graphene quantum dots, for drug delivery, cancer therapy, bio-sensing, antibacterials, antiviral and tissue engineering applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Selecting suitable materials for drug delivery, cancer treatment, bio-sensing, tissue engineering, regenerative medicine, antibacterial, and antiviral applications, as well as diagnostic purposes has been a challenge for biomedical researchers [1–4]. Carbon is a ubiquitous component of both nature and the human body. It possesses a remarkable ability to bind with different types of elements. Therefore, in the science and technology domain, carbon-based nanomaterials (CBNs) are emerging as highly intriguing nanomaterials with growing significance and appealing properties for many biomedical applications [5,6]. This is attributed to their varied architectures, high surface areas to volume ratios, easy functionalization, distinctive optical features, and biological compatibility [7,8]. They contribute significantly to showcasing impressive biological effectiveness by aiding the transport of therapeutic compounds and enabling the visualization of cells and tissues. These attributes are essential for addressing and treating diseased and damaged tissues [9]. Furthermore, the presence of a wide range of carbon allotropes, spanning from familiar phases like amorphous carbon, graphite, and nanodiamonds (NDs) to newly discovered promising materials such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene oxide (GO), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon dots (CDs) and fullerenes, has added significant value to carbon-based materials [10–13]. The categorization of carbon-based nanomaterials is conventionally based on the number of dimensions surpassing the nanoscale (100 nm). Consequently, carbon dots (CDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and fullerenes fall under the classification of zero-dimensional nanomaterials (0-D). Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are recognized as one-dimensional nanomaterials (1-D), while layered structures such as graphene are classified as two-dimensional nanomaterials (2-D) [10,14]. On the other hand, nanodiamonds are designated as three-dimensional carbon nanostructures (3-D), Fig. 1 .

Focusing on the several possible applications of CBNs in the biomedical sector are diverse and include bioimaging contrast agents, fluorescence labeling of cells, stem cell engineering, biosensing, drug and gene delivery, cell and tissue imaging, and phototherapy, encompassing both photothermal therapy (PTT) as well as photodynamic therapy (PDT) [15,16]. Recent studies have highlighted their potential utility in anticancer and anti-inflammatory treatments, Fig. 2
. For instance, carbon-based nanomaterials induce the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon internalization by cancerous cells, causing damage to lipids and DNA, ultimately resulting in cell death [17]. Moreover, graphene materials influence the metabolic activity of macrophages, elevating ROS levels and causing damage to the mitochondrial membrane, leading to cell death through apoptosis [18].

CBNs continue to present specific challenges in the biomedical realms, confronting several hurdles. Primary challenges are ensuring their biocompatibility and safety, obtaining regulatory approvals, and optimizing delivery efficiency for therapeutic agents. Additionally, maintaining stability, minimizing immunogenicity, and developing sensitive imaging techniques are crucial tasks, Fig. 3
. Conversely, when carbon-based nanomaterials are biofunctionalized, they improve delivery efficacy by reducing clearance and enhancing retention in the body [19]. Many alternative methods have been employed to characterize the carbon-based nanomaterials. Raman spectroscopy, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), energy dispersive X-ray diffraction (EDX), X-ray diffraction (XRD), atomic force microscopy (AFM), nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), laser scanning microscopy (LSM), electrochemistry, and conductivity measurements are all examples of such characterizing techniques [20,17].

Numerous comprehensive review articles have delved into the collective body of research spanning the past two decades. This review comprehensively summarizes the latest advancements in carbon-based nanomaterials (CBNs) for biomedical applications over the past five years. Each CBN will be discussed individually to highlight its specific characteristics and biological prospects. Additionally, we provide insights into the field, anticipating that this review will guide researchers in designing advanced CBNs for enhanced biomedical applications.

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are a highly adaptable carbon structure, first identified in 1991 by Sumio Iijima. These materials have undergone thorough research and have been widely used in different applications such as enhancing the strength of materials, producing electrode materials, and designing components for nanoelectronics like biosensors [21,22]. They are even utilised in the field of biomedicine as drug carriers that can be activated remotely. Various methods can be employed to synthesize them, for instance, the electric-arc discharge, laser ablation, and catalytic chemical vapor deposition (CCVD) techniques.

CNTs can be defined as cylindrical structures composed of a single layer of graphene, which may have closed ends capped with fullerene. Concentric walls comprising a carbon nanotube (if greater than one) are a crucial element governing numerous properties [23]. Single-wall carbon nanotubes (SWCNT) typically have a narrow diameter ranging from 1 to 2 nm, while multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) can have an outside diameter of approximately 100 nm [24]. The addition of layers to MWCNTs unavoidably enhances the likelihood of defects, rendering them more susceptible to modification and functionalization. Nevertheless, this often leads to a deterioration in their physical characteristics. Double-wall carbon nanotubes (DWCNT) are located at the SWCNT and MWCNT boundaries. DWCNT possesses several traits of SWCNT, such as a remarkably small diameter and exceptional mechanical properties [25]. However, similar to the MWCNT, they can undergo covalent functionalization without significantly compromising their electrical conductivity due to the existence of an additional outer wall. Undoubtedly, the significance of surface chemistry in nanoparticles, including carbon nanotubes (CNT), is a critical aspect to consider [26].

It is well-established that nanoparticle’s inherent chemical composition and crystal structure impact several surface properties, including charge, hydrophobicity/hydrophilicity, potential dissolution, and (photo)catalytic activity. Specifically, this will make the nanoparticle attach to proteins (corona) and connect with its surroundings [27]. Conversely, it has been shown that modifying the surface embellishment of nanoparticles can alter their surface properties and result in a distinct biological behaviour, significantly affecting their biodistribution. Comparable findings have also been documented for carbon nanotubes (CNT), which will be thoroughly examined in the subsequent section. Often, CNTs undergo covalent functionalization through oxidation using nitric acid (HNO3) alone or in combination with sulfuric acid (H2SO4) [28]. This process results in the attachment of oxygen-containing functional groups (such as hydroxyl and carboxylic acid) to the outer wall surface of the CNT and the creation of openings in the CNT structure [29]. However, in several other instances, the functionalization occurs by non-covalent means, specifically through the process of adsorption of various types of molecules such as polymers, DNA, carbohydrates, and their derivatives. Its exceptional mechanical and electrical properties make it ideal for sensing, medical diagnosis, and treatment of a wide range of ailments, and its structural and electrical diversity makes it adaptable to a wide range of biological entities [30].

Fullerenes, another molecular allotrope of carbon was first discovered in 1985. Fullerenes are carbon nanomaterials that are sp2-hybridized and consist of fused rings of five to seven carbon atoms. These rings are connected by single and double bonds, forming a closed or partially closed mesh structure. Originally, they were buckminster fullerenes, a novel type of carbon-only molecules with a soccer-ball-like arrangement of 60 carbon atoms (C60) [31]. They are also known as “bucky balls” and exist as C60, C70, etc. Typically, a fullerene or buckyball molecule has the form of a hollow sphere, ellipsoid, or tube. Thay are typically prepared by arc discharge method. When compared to other carbon nanostructures, fullerenes have the highest symmetry and are hence the most chemically and structurally stable. CNTs are fullerenes that resemble tubes or cylindrical structures, have exceptional strength, distinctive electrical properties, and are effective heat conductors. Carbon atoms are arranged in hexagonal rings in CNTs, which also have pentagonal rings to create a curve. Carbon nanotubes are essentially split into two groups: single-walled nanotubes (SWNTs) and multi-walled nanotubes (MWNTs). Single-walled nanotubes have a diameter of less than 1, whereas multi-walled nanotubes have diameters of up to 100 nm and are composed of many concentrically interconnected nanotubes. Their length might range from a few micrometers to millimeters [32]. Different functional groups can be added to fullerene surfaces to enable biomedical applications such as imaging, antioxidant and anticancer activity, biosensing, diagnostics, tissue engineering, drug/gene delivery, and antimicrobial therapeutics. It can also be used in cryoprotection, enzyme inhibition, and biocidal activity [33,34]. Because of their small size of approximately less than 1 nm, both unmodified and modified fullerenes can aggregate at the cell membrane or enter intracellular space, endangering the integrity and functionality of cells.

Nanodiamonds, commonly referred to as diamond nanoparticles, have garnered significant attention in the field of biomedical engineering in recent times. These single-crystal diamonds exhibit remarkable physical and chemical properties because of their carbon composition. As we know diamond has been known to exist from prehistoric times, just like graphite. Diamond is a meta-stable carbon allotrope with carbon atoms arranged in a face-centered cubic crystal structure [35]. NDs are well-known for being ultra dispersed diamonds with unique diamond core characteristics, including strong hardness, exceptional thermal conductivity, and a chemically inert core. NDs can be synthesized in several ways, including chemical vapour deposition, high temperature, high pressure, and explosive detonation. Diamond formation occurs at a pressure of 5–6 Gpa and temperatures ranging between 900–1400 degrees [36]. Although diamond is less stable than graphite, it can easily convert to graphite under normal circumstances. The presence of additional elements in diamonds, such as nitrogen and boron, affects their colour. Diamond is thought to have the maximum heat conductivity of any material. Low phonon scattering and strong covalent bonds that held the atoms together are definitively attributable to this. Reportedly, the heat conductivity of natural diamond is approximately 2200 W/(mK), making it five times more potent than copper [37]. Due to its exceptional thermal conductivity, it is commonly employed in the semiconductor sector to prevent overheating of silicon and other semiconducting materials [38].

The next generation of pharmaceuticals and drug delivery agents is quickly realizing the potential of nanodiamonds (NDs). They possess a large surface area and unique electrostatic fields, making them suitable for conjugating entities such as genes and drugs for intracellular and extracellular delivery [39]. They exhibit high surface modifiability and biocompatibility. Furthermore, the surface of nanodiamonds contains a range of functional groups that enable covalent and hydrogen bonding with therapeutic molecules, including hydroxyl, pyrone, phenol, and epoxide groups to be used in biological and medical fields. Additionally, the fluorescence-based diagnostic capabilities of NDs enhance their value as bio-imaging probes [40].

Graphene is a recently emerged nanomaterial. In 2004, Geim and Novoselov conducted pioneering research introducing a straightforward technique for obtaining graphene from graphite through exfoliation [41]. Additionally, they investigated the distinctive electrical properties of graphene. Graphene consists of a single sheet of densely packed sp2 carbon atoms. Graphene and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) share similar optical, electrical, and thermal properties. However, graphene's unique two-dimensional atomic structure allows for a broader range of electronic characteristics. This includes the presence of the quantum Hall effect and massless Dirac fermions, which help explain the low-energy charge excitation at room temperature and the optical transparency in the infrared and visible parts of the electromagnetic spectrum [42]. Graphene has a planer thickness of 0.77 mg/m2, making it a very light material. Graphene synthesis can be accomplished either top-down or bottom-up. Several synthesis processes, including mechanical exfoliation, intercalation, reduction of graphite oxide (rGO), and nanotube slicing, are included in the top-down approach. The bottom-up methods for preparing graphene include pyrolysis, chemical vapor deposition (CVD), and graphene epitaxial growth.

Graphene can be dissolved in water and other solvents after undergoing chemical functionalization. It has excellent electrical and thermal conductivity in addition to its remarkable optical characteristics. Additionally, it possesses strong structural integrity while maintaining a high level of flexibility, rendering it appealing for developing thin and flexible materials in the engineering field [43]. Graphene has no subsurface atoms; hence, it can serve as a substrate for various biomaterials. Such characteristics make graphene a promising material for drug delivery, tissue engineering, genetic engineering, biosensing, bioimaging, and therapies [44].

The single-atomic layer material, graphene oxide (GO), is made from the readily available and inexpensive mineral graphite. Groups containing oxygen are woven into an oxidized form of graphene, known as graphene oxide, and exhibit a range of physico-chemical properties. It is a thick honeycomb-like 2D single-atom structure that is in fact a hydrophilic variant of graphene [45]. It is expected to be straightforward to produce because it disperses in water and can be utilized to make graphene (as well as other solvents). The conductivity of graphene oxide can be increased, though it is not a particularly effective conductor. It is usually provided for commercial use as a dispersion, coating on surfaces, or in powder form. There are generally four primary methods to synthesize graphene oxide: Staudenmaier, Hofmann, Brodie, and Hummer’s method [46]. Strong acids, such as sulfuric or nitric and potassium chlorate, are applied using the Brodie, Staudenmaier, and Hoffman techniques. Hummer’s most widely used technique includes integrating concentrated H2SO4, NaNO3, and KMnO4. Compared to earlier approaches, Hummer’s technique offers a good yield and takes less time. Yellow-colored GO particles suspended in water are the end result of the GO synthesis process [47]. These approaches come in a variety of forms, and new innovations are constantly being researched to offer more effective processes at lower costs. The carbon/oxygen ratios of graphene oxide are commonly employed to assess the effectiveness of an oxidation process. Graphene oxide (GO) is a crucial and auspicious substance with a wide range of uses in electronics, optics, chemistry, energy storage, and biology [48].

Compared with other applications, GO is more suitable for biomedical applications due to its biocompatibility and its shape may be modified. The Raman signals in the electromagnetic spectrum's D, G, and 2D sections and its fluorescence in the visible and infrared make it a valuable tool for bioimaging and biosensing. Furthermore, GO's aqueous stability raises serious questions regarding its potential use in drug administration. Due to non-specific binding and electrostatic interactions, GO tends to aggregate in physiological solutions containing proteins and salt, hindering biological probes' development. Through precise functionalization of graphene oxide (GO), its physiological solubility can be improved, maximizing its potential for biological applications [49].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are tiny fragments of graphene that exhibit excitation confinement in particles ranging from 3 to 20 nm. They are considered a novel type of zero-dimensional anisotropic luminous nanomaterials, displaying a quantum-size effect [50].As colloidal semiconductor nanocrystals, GQDs possess quantum confinement properties and emit fluorescence across a spectrum of visible to infrared (IR) wavelengths when excited. GQDs, due to their exceptional biocompatibility, low-cost preparation methods, and remarkable optoelectronic abilities, can potentially replace the more widely known metal chalcogenides-based quantum dots [51]. Furthermore, in contrast to traditional semiconductor quantum dots, graphene exhibits remarkable thermal and electrical conductivity due to the π–π bonding above and below the atomic plane. This allows graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to maintain their beneficial properties without worrying about inherent toxicity. GQD’s optical properties are highly dependent on their size due to the quantum-confinement effect and variations in the density and kind of sp2 sites that are available in them. As a result, tuning the size of GQDs can alter their energy band gap.

In the past couple of decades, quantum dots have gained significant attention and achieved notable advancements in the field of nanomaterials. However, graphene-based nanohybrids have garnered more interest and fascination in scientific research in recent years. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have experienced significant growth as versatile instruments in numerous scientific domains, such as photonics, composites, energy, and electronics, due to their exceptional mechanical characteristics, biological compatibility, transparency, and electrical conductivity [52]. Currently, GQDs-based nanomaterials have demonstrated outstanding potential in several biomedical applications, specifically in diagnostics, drug administration, near-infrared (NIR) light-induced photothermal therapy, biosensing, cancer therapies, as well as in vitro and in vivo bioimaging [53]. Table 1 summarizes various carbon-based nanomaterials utilized in the biomedical sector. It highlights the characteristics that render them advantageous for applications in tissue engineering, drug delivery, cancer therapy, antiviral and antibacterial functions, and biosensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of carbon-based nanomaterials in biomedical Sciences

ce:section.ce:para:
The utilization of diverse nanomaterials, including liposomes, polymersomes, microspheres, and polymer conjugates, as carriers for delivering therapeutic agents has significantly advanced drug delivery due to the advancements in nanotechnology. Of all the carbon allotropes, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have gained more significant recognition as a very competent vehicle for carrying diverse therapeutic compounds into living cells due to their intrinsic shape, allowing for non-invasive penetration across biological membranes. Since CNTs have been demonstrated to physically adsorb and interact with a wide range of biomacromolecules, including DNA and proteins, they have also been the subject of intense study as drug delivery vehicles. Typically, pharmacological molecules are bonded to the surfaces of CNTs, either by covalent or non-covalent bonding with the functionalized CNTs. It is well known that surface functionalization of CNTs can improve cellular uptake by increasing permeability and retention, a process known as the “EPR effect” that causes nanoparticles to accumulate in tumors as a result of increased blood flow, and selective targeting by a specific antigen or aptamer. Moreover, CNT modification also disrupts the cell membrane integrity, leading to rapid cellular uptake, Fig. 4
.

A non-covalent modification approach entails encasing CNTs in a long-chain polymer, such as polystyrene sulfonate. In contrast, CNTs in covalent or chemical modification have been enhanced by creating a covalent bond between CNTs and coupling agents. This change could reduce the possible mechanical strength of functionalized CNTs. The degree to which the purifying process increased varied according to the oxidation conditions [27]. Applications for cell labeling in T1-weighted MRI were studied using functionalized MWCNTs. In this instance, the gadolinium (Gd) chelating agent and MWCNTs were covalently coupled, helping to ensure that Gd atoms were tightly attached to the nanotube surface. These substances were discovered to be stable in water and mouse serum for two weeks. The material was shown to have a higher r1 relaxivity than the clinically approved T1 of Magnevist [65]. As a result of these findings, functionalized CNTs have emerged as a promising novel delivery technology for delivering drugs to several mammalian cell types [66]. It will be essential to study these CNT conjugate’s metabolism, biodistribution, and elimination for future advancement, notwithstanding their low cytotoxicity in vitro.

The diverse characteristics of carbon nanotubes have led to their extensive use in biomedical applications. They are the most promising candidates for transporting genes, proteins, and anticancer drugs associated with chemotherapy. CNTs have a high absorption capacity of near-infrared (NIR) light. As a result, they can function effectively as photothermal agents. In the research by Su et al. (2017), MWCNT functionalized with iRGD-polyethyleneimine (PEI) was developed that was subsequently conjugated with candesartan (CD). It was the functionalized iRGD-PEI-MWCNT-CD that was constructed using plasmid AT (2) [pAT (2)]. They employed iRGD and CD to target the αvβ3-integrin of tumor endothelium and AT1R of lung cancer cells. The CD chemotherapy showed synergistic downregulation of VEFG and successfully reduced angiogenesis when administered with pAT (2), Fig. 5
A [67]. In 2017, the Zhang group developed a nanocomposite consisting of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) embedded with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and loaded with DOX. This nanocomposite was designed for applications in photothermal therapy and chemotherapy. The GdN@CQDs-MWCNTs were able to interact with positively charged DOX molecules because of their negatively charged surface. The material's absorption of near-infrared light was relatively high. Using in vivo photothermal therapy with laser irradiation of power density (2 W/cm2) for five minutes, the tumor site temperature of GdN@CQDs-MWCNTs/DOX-EGFR ( Fig. 5 B) treated mice was elevated to 51.8 °C. There was no discernible rise or fall in temperature at the tumor treatment site in the control group of mice. This heating action facilitated DOX release and photothermal treatment, as the tumor volume lowered [68]. Combining hyaluronic acid (HA) with chlorine6 (Ce6) on the surface of SWCNTs enabled Sundaram and Abrahamse to examine the nanocomposite's toxicity on colon cancer cells by photodynamic treatment (PDT). They demonstrated that colon cancer cells exposed to SWCNTs-HA-Ce6 died much faster after PDT [69].

A biosensor is a cutting-edge medical diagnostic tool that transforms a biological or physical event into a discernible signal. To date, biosensors are effectively employed in managing human health, detecting diseases, preventing diseases, rehabilitating patients, and monitoring their health. Microorganisms such as bacteria, viruses, and pathogens can also be found using biosensors [70]. Typically, a biosensor is made up of a number of sections or elements, including an analyzer, a bioreceptor, a transducer, electronics, and a reader display. The related electronics or signal processors are typically connected to the display reader device utilized in the biosensor to display the results. Readers are usually manufactured and precisely tailored to match the different operating principles of biosensors. Sometimes, this component is the most expensive aspect of the development of the sensors. To quantify the stimulus as output data, the transducer logs the data and converts it into an electrical signal while the sensor analyses the consumer in response to various stimuli [71].

ce:section.ce:section-title: CNTs in antibacterial and antiviral activity
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of covalently functional groups or chemicals that are adsorbed on the surface of nanotubes has the potential to significantly influence the reactions of microorganisms, which in turn offers antibacterial and antiviral activity [78]. A study demonstrated by Hassani et al. 2022 found, through in vitro and in vivo investigations, that a functionalized carbon nanotube-levofloxacin combination had robust antibacterial activity. It acts as a fast-acting, safe, and effective nanodrug with the fewest possible adverse effects, creating a new nano-antibiotic known as MWCNT-LVX [79]. Several investigations have demonstrated the effective bacteriostatic effects of composites, including MWCNTs and silver nanoparticles, against S. aureus, P. aeruginosa, and Escherichia coli; this finding may be associated with a strong synergistic impact of the two substances. They also demonstrated nanocomposites made of low-density polyethylene and MWCNTs, which have antibacterial effects against Escherichia coli [80]. Nevertheless, according to Ding and colleague's studies, oxidized MWCNTs dispersed singly had better antibacterial activity than aggregated raw MWCNTs. Their research shows that the antibacterial impact of CNTs is dependent on the dispersion of MWCNTs [81]. Additionally, we may say that the hybrid nanomaterials considerably enhance antibacterial activity against Klebsiella pneumonia and E. coli, according to Spizzirri et al., who produced bioconjugates including fluoroquinolones, gelatin, and MWCNTs [82].

Moreover, on the antiviral activity of the CNTs, fishes infected with the nervous necrosis virus (NNV) cause a disease called viral nervous necrosis disease that shows compromised central nervous systems (CNS), posing a technical challenge in the effort to restore damaged tissue. In increasing the effectiveness of isoprinosine administration, fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) and isoprinosine, an anti-NNV agent, were coupled with SWCNTs. When a delivery system consisting of isoprinosine, bovine serum albumin, and SWCNTs was studied in zebrafish larvae in vitro, it demonstrated improved antiNNV activity with a markedly lower mortality rate [83] . Another application of SWCNTs was to enhance the efficiency of a bath vaccine against iridovirus in juvenile pearl gentian groupers. When SWCNTs were used as a vaccine carrier, the immune genes were more effectively delivered [84].

Fullerenes have been the subject of both computational and experimental investigations on their potential as drug delivery carriers. The compact dimensions of these fullerene nanostructures facilitate effortless conjugation with drug molecules. Moreover, the nanostructures possess surface properties and a non-toxic nature, enabling them to transport chemotherapeutics specifically to targeted cells without impacting healthy cells. As a result, they are highly valued as optimal delivery carriers. In 2022, Bibi et al. employed boron nitride fullerenes as a carrier for delivering the anticancer medicine gemcitabine. They utilised the B3LYP/6-31G (d, p) level of DFT theory for their study. This study investigated gemcitabine's adsorption energy and electrical properties when it interacts with metal-doped BN nanostructures. The drug-delivery capabilities of BN nanostructures are greatly improved by metal doping, according to the observations which led to conclusion that fullerenes can be a potential candidate for delivery of drugs in treatment [93]. The US Food and Drug Administration has approved the use of the drug paclitaxel for cancer treatment, with a focus on drug delivery. Due to its water solubility, the fullerene derivative enables drug absorption without the need for a nonaqueous solvent, which could be harsh for the patient [94]. However, fullerenes encounter two significant challenges: their limited solubility and the risk of inducing oxidative stress in the brain. Although the investigation was carried out on non-functionalized fullerenes, Zhu et al. found that fullerenes can lower glutathione levels and enhance oxidative stress in the brain [95]. In another study, Zakharian et al. proposed a drug delivery system containing fullerene and paclitaxel, with a range of approximately 120–145 nm, aiming to perform aerosol liposome administration of paclitaxel for lung cancer treatment. Paclitaxel was released into the bloodstream by enzymatic hydrolysis; it had an 80-minute half-life in bovine serum [96]. One major advantage of drug delivery systems based on fullerene is their capacity to transport various pharmacological payloads, such as Taxol and other chemotherapy medications. Because cancer cells are so good at developing resistance to drugs and effective treatment typically requires a combination of many drugs to circumvent this resistance mechanism, this strategy is beneficial in cancer treatment [97].

Fullerenes show a dual role as free-radical scavengers and cytotoxic free-radical generators when exposed to light, which opens up exciting new possibilities for photodynamic therapy (PDT) in treating cancer. It is well known that photoexcitation of C60 from its ground state can lead to a transition from a short-lived singlet state to 1C60, and then to a long-lived triplet state to 3C60. From there, energy can be transferred to peripheral oxygen, resulting in the production of singlet oxygen. Furthermore, the electron transfer makes the reduction of 1C60 and 3C60 to C60− easy. Free radicals with a strong affinity for various biomolecules, such as lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids, are the key to the fullerene’s mechanism of action. Similar to conventional PDT, this reaction mechanism efficiently kills cells and helps in cancer therapy [98]. The landmark study by Iwamoto et al. showed that C60, when solubilized by covalent attachment of N-vinylpyrrolidone (NVP), had exceptional ability to generate O2 •− and cleave DNA when exposed to visible light. This suggests that C60 has great potential for photodynamic treatment [99]. This work established the enormous prospect of employing functionalized fullerenes for photodynamic therapy (PDT) of tumors, as several of these fullerenes have demonstrated exceptional capabilities in photoirradiation-induced toxicity to cells and animal tumor xenografts [100,101]. A study in the year 2016 by Jinjin et al. identified fullerene (C60)-based tumor-targeting nanoparticles featuring an “off-on” mode to improve cancer treatment. This research indicates that both in vivo and in vitro studies demonstrate the C60-based drug delivery system, characterized by an “off-on” state, possesses significant antitumor efficacy and minimal toxicity to normal tissues, attributable to its tumor-targeting capability, remote-controlled drug release feature, and synergistic therapeutic effect of photodynamic therapy combined with chemotherapy [102] Fig. 8 .

There has been a lot of research into fullerenes (C60), their substitutes, and composites as electrochemical sensors because of their biocompatibility, high surface-to-volume ratios, facile chemical modification, and increased electron-transfer kinetics. They have been utilized in electrochemical biosensors to increase their sensitivity, selectivity, and reproducibility because of their capacity to decrease the potential of electro-reduction of redox substrates and raise reaction rates [103]. Recently a polymer containing fullerene (C60) was created by coupling a benzoxadiazole core with thienopyrroledione and benzodithiophene; the enzyme was then immobilized onto this matrix. For the first time, a biosensing architecture based on a mix of fullerene and poly[BDT-alt-(TP;BO)] polymer was explicitly created for catechol detection by Kurbanoglu et al., 2022 [104]. This material combination has the potential to be used to generate catechol biosensors that are both extremely sensitive and have a rapid response time. Hernandez et al., for example, created a low-cost electrochemical technology using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) methodologies to quantify ciprofloxacin in beef samples using a carbon paste electrode modified with nafion and fullerene (N-F/CPE). The suggested methodology's analytical performance yielded a repeatability of 5.38 percent, an LOD of 1.0 µM, and an LOQ of 3.0 µM [105].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fullerenes in antibacterial and antiviral activity
ce:section.ce:para: Fullerenes have garnered significant interest due to their potential application in human therapeutics. It is a spherically caged all-carbon nanomolecule that differs from conventional small drug molecules [106]. Due to their distinctive physical and chemical attributes, fullerenes and their derivatives have shown considerable potential as antibacterial and antiviral drugs. With the inclusion of metallic nanoparticles, fullerenes have shown impactful antibacterial efficacy. Most recently, Pan et al. utilised ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), and liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC-MS) to investigate the antibacterial mechanism of a synthesised silver-depositedfullerene material (Ag(I)–C60) against Staphylococcus aureus. These techniques were utilized to understand the creation of radical species, changes in silver shape and content, oxidation products of DNA base, and the transformation routes involved in their formation [107]. With regard to the antibacterial activity of Ag(I)–C60 material against S. aureus, it has been discovered that the release of Ag+ in water and the activation of ROS generation caused by light irradiation are the primary results. In another study conducted by Ansari and colleagues, C60 was functionalized to produce fullerenol. This fullerenol was then loaded with sulfasalazine (SSZ) to explore its potential uses in the realm of biomedicine. For both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, the results of the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) tests demonstrated that SSZ/fullerenol was effective in preventing the growth of these bacteria [108].

Most importantly, fullerenes demonstrate potent antiviral activity too that inhibits virus reproduction both in vitro and in vivo including HIV, herpes simplex virus (HSV), influenza virus, Ebola virus, cytomegalovirus (CMV), Hepatitis C Virus (HCV), and other viruses [109,94,110]. It has been shown that fullerenes have a powerful effect on HCV by inhibiting enzymes that are bound to cell membrane replication and protease enzymes. However, the primary problem with fullerenes is that they are not very soluble and their production costs are significantly greater. The production of pyridinium-type fullerene derivatives that inhibited HIV enzymes was demonstrated in a study that Kobayashi and colleagues conducted. Fullerenes have proven to be remarkably effective against HIV and HCV. They work in tandem by going after two particular enzymes connected to these viruses. Protease (HIVP) and reverse transcriptase (HIV-RT) are the enzymes that are specific to HIV [111]. Fullerenes have a strong effect on HIV and HCV, and their non-toxic nature makes them even more effective at preventing HCV infection [112].

Biomedical scaffolds are man-made structures that can be implanted within or applied externally to the body. Tissue engineering relies heavily on biomaterials because they can modulate cell development, impact cell–cell interactions, and drive specific biological processes. With the development of scaffolds that promote cell adhesion and proliferation, tissue engineering has emerged as a significant alternative for patients requiring tissue regeneration due to injury or loss. The discovery of improved cell adherence to fullerene biomaterials has recently sparked a lot of interest in bone tissue engineering developments. Carbon nanofibers coated with fullerene were synthesized by Bacakova et al. The nanofibers demonstrated a 4.5-fold increase in cell proliferation within 7 days and enhanced the adhesion of osteoblastic MG 63 cells [113]. Microarrays based on fullerene were created in this study utilizing a metallic “nanomask” to improve the attachment and proliferation of MG 63 osteoblast bone cells. Layered surface architecture is essential for cell proliferation since cells are mostly between groove prominences. However, due to fullerene's hydrophobicity, fullerene-based biomaterial limited cell height to 1 mm. In an additional investigation, this team advanced the idea that carbon nanoparticles and complements could treat bone problems caused by arthritis. Findings indicated that fullerene materials were stable, did not damage DNA, and had no effect on the morphology of osteoblastic MG 63 and U-2 OS cells. On the other hand, they may improve bone cells' biological function [114].

In recent times, nanodiamonds (NDs) with their abundant surface functional groups and enormous surface area have been proposed as promising material for conjugation of active therapeutic ingredients. The only criteria considered while selecting a drug carrier are its loading capacity, environmental protection capabilities, and inertness. A substantial drug loading efficiency at a lower carrier concentration is very desirable. To produce the intended therapeutic effect, a drug must release from the carrier rapidly [115]. Huang et al. conducted a study that specifically examined the loading and release of the anticancer drug doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) from the NDs. The premise of the study was that NDs and DOX may form a loose cluster due to ionic interactions between their carboxylic and hydroxylic groups and amine group of DOX drug. Subsequent studies, stated that DOX drug was adsorbed onto the ND's surface and within the crevices of the cluster.

Furthermore, assessments of DOX-ND’s cytotoxicity on mouse macrophages and human colorectal cancer cells indicated a reduced harmful impact with a prolonged release compared to free DOX [116]. In addition to their huge conjugation surface area, NDs have recently gained popularity as agents that improve the dispersibility of hydrophobic medicines [117]. Some chemotherapeutic drugs such as purvalanol A, used for liver cancer, and 4-hydroxytamoxifen, used for breast cancer, have limited ability to be delivered through injections due to their solubility in organic solvents [118,119]. Because NDs adsorb drugs on surfaces while keeping their therapeutic efficacy, they have the property of improving dispersibility in water. The MTT assay validated the therapeutic activity of the NDs formulations. In light of these results, NDs may be useful in the future formulation of poorly water-soluble medications [120].

There are several pathways that the nanoparticles can mediate or impact, which allows the medicine to be delivered and accumulate in the tumour. Passive tumour targeting, which is a kind of the latter, is exemplified by the EPR effect. Since EPR depends on a fully developed tumour vasculature, it can only be applied later in carcinogenesis. But new evidence suggests that ND can increase vascular permeability even in the absence of tumor-induced EPR. NDs have the ability to transport biological molecules to specific cells, making them a popular tool in the fight against cancer. Man et al. created daunorubicin-NDs conjugates, assisting the treatment of chemoresistant leukemia with increase in therapy efficacy, particularly against resistant types. In order to prove that the treatment was more effective, researchers used a human myelogenous leukemia cell line called K562, which is resistant to many drugs and has had increased exposure to daunorubicin [121]. In a study, ND conjugated with DOX, an antitumor drug demonstrated the delayed release over the course of a month, making it an effective cancer-targeted drug vector [122]. In a different investigation, the ND-DOX conjugates were found to prevent the growth of tumours more effectively than free DOX [123]. Moreover, the therapeutic effects were also found to be consistent across a variety of cancer models. The antitumor activity of the conjugates of ND hydroxycamptothecin (HCPT) and daunorubicin has been significantlyincreased [121]. Research was conducted on the appropriate combination of NDs with gold/silver nanoparticles for the purpose of photothermal therapy in cancer [124]. Nowadays multifunctional nanodiamond hydrogels are also constructed for its application in wound healing and cancer therapy.

Biosensors based nanoparticles are crucial for the early identification of disease, but their sensitivity is frequently inadequate. Nowadays, fluorescent nanodiamonds have become popular for in vitro biosensing due to their selective emission controllability, low cost, and brightness. In a report by Miller et al., demonstrated the use of fluorescent nanodiamonds as an ultrasensitive label for in vitro diagnostics. He used frequency-domain analysis to isolate the signal from background autofluorescence, which normally limits sensitivity, and a microwave field to adjust emission intensity. From this finding we discovered a biotin-avidin model with a limit of detection of 8.2 × 10−19 M, which is 105 times greater than what was accomplished with gold nanoparticles, by utilizing the widely used and economical lateral flow format as an example [125]. Another study from Wang et al., (2018) created a stimuli-responsive metastasis detection complex using nanodiamonds. They found that increased production of matrix metalloproteinase 9 (MMP9) is a good indicator of metastasis. Nanodiamonds that have been functionalized with substrate peptides that are specific to MMP9 and are fluorescently labelled make up the nanodiamond-MMP9 biosensor complex. This layout allows for the precise measurement of MMP9 protease activity, which may then be linked to MMP9 expression. In addition, the nanodiamond-MMP9 biosensor showed improved protection of the basic sensor peptide against nonspecific serum protease cleavage. There is compelling evidence to support the creation of a nanodiamond-MMP9 biosensor for metastatic site identification, thanks to the improved peptide stability and a quantifiable stimuli-responsive output function [126].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanodiamonds in antibacterial and antiviral activity
ce:section.ce:para: It is interesting to note that nanodiamond materials have also been researched to possibly exhibit antibacterial characteristics. In addition, the relatively rare combination of nanodiamond's like biocompatibility and antibacterial activity would further boost its acceptability as a substrate for the production of biomaterials [127]. This would be a significant advantage. Nonetheless, various considerations must be made to protect an environment from bacterial colonization and growth effectively. A variety of approaches have been studied for using nanodiamonds as an antibacterial substance. However, there is significant disagreement regarding the results. Most studies have focused on detonation nanodiamonds (DNDs), which are spherical particles with a diameter of approximately 5 nm generated through the detonation of carbon-rich explosives [35]. These have been used with their original form and varied surface alterations [128]. Given this, nanodiamonds (NDs) treated with glycans have shown tremendous potential as antimicrobial nanostructures [129]. For example, Beranova et al. studied the effect of as-synthesized ND particles on the growth of Gram-negative bacteria, E. coli. They found that the presence of NDs on agar plates significantly hindered the capacity of E. coli to form colonies compared to when NDs were absent. The antibacterial impact was observed in a manner that was dependent on concentration, although not linear. A 5 µg/mL concentration of NDs resulted in a 25% reduction of the number of colonies on LB agar plates. However, to achieve a growth inhibition of 100%, it was necessary to reach concentrations that were greater than 50 µg/mL. In order to explain the antibacterial effect of NDs, it was anticipated that the clumping of NDs around bacterial cells would inhibit key biological operations [130]. A more recent study by Wheling and colleagues examined the impact of surface chemical termination on the antibacterial capabilities of NDs in great depth. Negatively charged NDs were highly effective against E. coli and B. subtilis bacteria, whereas positively charged NDs didn't kill bacteria until they reached very high quantities [131]. A hypothesis suggests that the acid anhydride groups, which are negatively charged and partially oxidized on the surface of ND particles, may be responsible for their antibacterial action. Further research is urgently required to investigate the potential of these ND structures as anti-adhesives to prevent bacterial colonization and infection in living organisms. A comprehensive understanding of the antibacterial characteristics of ND particles necessitates such exploration. While there is still a considerable distance to cover, the future holds significant promise for ND particles in combating bacterial infections.

Through the application of nanotechnology, it has become feasible to understand the cellular mechanics of living cells and to develop technologies that are potentially useful in the early diagnosis and treatment of a wide range of viral infections. Among all the CBNs, nanodiamonds also exhibits antiviral activity against various viruses. Based on the research carried out by Roy et al. in 2018, a single cART drug called efavirenz ((a non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor or NNRTI, EFV), was created using three distinct types of nanodiamonds: unmodified ND, also known as ND, –COOH surface modified ND, and –NH2 surface modified ND, all of which are utilised to treat HIV. Due to blood plasma protein binding, EFV has a poor bioavailability as a free medication, with a binding affinity of 99.5% [132–134]. If we want to improve drug delivery to the central nervous system, ND is a great material to use because of its tiny size and low toxicity. Further, functionalized ND can pass the Blood-Brain Barrier (BBB) to deliver anti-HIV-1 medications. Researchers have shown that conjugating EFV with functionalized ND will significantly boost its therapeutic efficiency by utilizing ND towards medication delivery to treat HIV-1 CNS reservoirs [135].

Furthermore, NDs and ND-ODA exhibited non-toxicity and biodegradability, making them viable alternatives in bone tissue engineering [140]. Parizek et al. developed ND-coated polymers for bone tissue engineering using electrospinning. Nanofibrous membranes with PLGA and NDs produced nanoparticles measuring 270 ± 9 nm, compared to 218 ± 4 nm for pure PLGA. PLGA-ND fibers had smaller, thicker structures and demonstrated high mechanical strength. They effectively supported the adhesion and growth of human osteoblast-like MG-63 cells, proving safe, non-toxic, and non-inflammatory [141].

Graphene, a very resourceful carbon nanomaterial, has been employed as an effective drug carrier to load substantial amounts of drug molecules onto both sides of the single atom layer sheet due to its extremely high surface area and sp2 hybridized carbon area. Despite graphene's promising future as a pharmaceutical carrier, its ability to aggregate in tissues and cause oxidative stress makes it harmful to human organs. It is crucial to modify the surface of graphene in order to circumvent this problem. The deep eutectic solvent (DES) technique allows for the attachment of several functional groups to the surface of graphene, which enables it to overcome these limitations. Graphene can now be used for more advanced drug delivery applications due to its functionalization. Drug carrier applications also benefit from graphene's enormous surface-area-to-volume ratio. Nanomaterials made up of graphene have special qualities like chemical and mechanical stability, two-dimensional geometries, and biocompatibility; therefore, they are used mainly in the field of cancer treatment.

Cancer theranostic applications suggest that photothermal agents, including nano-graphene or its derivatives with a high near-infrared (NIR) absorbance, could be helpful in cancer photothermal therapy (PTT). Owing to its extraordinarily large specific surface area, graphene is perfect for efficiently loading a diverse range of biomolecules, making it a great candidate for drug delivery applications. Binding inorganic nanoparticles onto the surface of nano-graphene can improve in vivo tumor multimodal imaging. The material's optical and magnetic properties would be enhanced, which could enhance contrast. In addition, 64Cu, 66Ga, 125I, and 131I functionalized nano-graphene with radionuclide labels may show promise in nuclear imaging and radiation treatment for cancer. Moreover, their extensive and easily modifiable surfaces allow them to be modified to interact with epoxide hydroxyl, carboxyl, and hydroxy (−O-, –COOH, –OH) groups. These groups can also be used to alter the surface characteristics of GO and serve as binding sites for a range of molecules, including proteins, DNA, and RNA. As a result, graphene oxide-based nanomaterials are well suited for use in various applications, such as bioimaging, phototherapy, and chemotherapeutic agent and gene delivery [142,143]. Numerous researchers have looked at the utilization of nano-drug delivery systems based on graphene materials, including Wu et al., who employed the graphene oxide conjugate with hyaluronic acid. Drug delivery against HeLa/L929 cells was successful according to this theory. Doxorubicin (DOX) was the drug used in this study. In order to successfully deliver the needed chemotherapeutic agent to the target, this drug was loaded onto graphene oxide-hyaluronic acid. Accordingly, the study demonstrated that graphene-HA might effectively deliver the medicine doxorubicin to tumors while also stymieing the growth of the tumor [144]. Another research group, Isiklan et al., developed a method for administering drugs and photothermal therapy against cancer. They created magnetic graphene oxide nanosheets coupled with gelatin-decorated MGO. They examined them using different characterization techniques such as Thermo gravimetric analysis, Brunauer–Emmett–Teller, Vibrating-sample magnetometer, Transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction analysis, and Atomic force microscopy. The chemotherapeutic drug Paclitaxel was released at the site of the tumor in this investigation using the developed platform (MGO@GEL), boosting its penetration into cancer cells. Calculations were made about the release of the PAC in response to 0.1 W/cm2 (808 nm) NIR laser light [145]. The research mentioned above demonstrated that the utilization of graphene and similar nanomaterials has a significant potential to produce an efficient drug.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene in antibacterial and antiviral activity
ce:section.ce:para:
Antibacterial materials are becoming increasingly important in everyday life to protect human health from various contaminants, such as organic, inorganic, and biological chemicals. Due to their numerous advantages compared to traditional antimicrobial substances, such as significant toxicity to bacteria and minimal cytotoxicity to mammals, graphene and its derivatives have garnered considerable attention as potential antibacterial materials [151]. Many hypotheses have been advanced to explain graphene's antibacterial abilities, including electron transport, oxidative stress, and membrane stress. In the realm of antibacterial materials, both unmodified and modified graphene serve as carriers for silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). In research led by Jiang et al., high-quality graphene nanosheets were coated with evenly distributed AgNPs, achieved through using sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) as a reducing agent. The composite material successfully suppressed the growth and proliferation of E. coli ( Fig. 11
A) [152]. Furthermore, the antibacterial graphene/AgNPs composite holds tremendous promise as a wound dressing after creating a porous hydrogel structure with the help of crosslinking agents. In addition, AgNPs can be transported on modified graphene nanosheets; these nanosheets were synthesized on the surface of graphene-grafted poly (acrylic acid) (PAA) without requiring an extra reductant or extensive treatment. The results of this finding were excellent due to the antibacterial effect of silver nanoparticles and modified graphene, elucidating the antibacterial efficacy of graphene nanomaterials ( Fig. 11 B) [153].

Graphene not only conjugates with antiviral chemicals but also directly interacts with viruses. It can potentially influence the viral envelope through electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions with the virus [154]. Matharu et al. revealed a recently discovered direct contact mechanism. The antiviral property of graphene nanoplatelets, measuring 110 nm by 170 nm, was examined through a 24-hour incubation period of a graphene suspension in the double-stranded DNA virus Escherichia coli T4 bacteriophage. By the end of three hours, the virion population had drastically decreased, and the graphene nanoplatelets had utterly stopped the infection. They hypothesized that the form of graphene and the reactive oxygen species (ROS) it generated were responsible for its antiviral capabilities [155]. According to another recent study, a hybrid material composed of graphene and copper was discovered to prevent influenza's spread effectively. After attaching itself to the host cell, the virus has already entered the cell through the dissolution of its envelope approximately thirty minutes after the attachment [156]. The antiviral properties of graphene can be combined with negatively charged antivirals, such as sulphates containing negative charges [157]. The antiviral activity of graphene was improved through the functionalization of graphene, which Donskyi and several coworkers carried out. Synthesis of these components made it possible to produce the conjugation of graphene derivatives with polyglycerol sulphate and alkyl chains (C3–C18). On the other hand, graphene with the C12 alkyl chain showed a high amount of toxicity toward Vero cells despite being the most effective HSV-1 inhibitor for Vero cells. Even though the chains that were shorter than C12 did not cause any harm to Vero cells, they successfully decreased the levels of HSV-1 [158].

One of the most popular approaches utilized in bone tissue engineering is to replicate the natural processes of bone remodeling and regeneration. To implement three-dimensional strategies, it is essential to use biocompatible scaffolds, biodegradable materials, and osteoinductive or osteoconductive techniques. Such a scaffold would create an environment conducive to mimicking the extracellular matrix, supporting osteogenic cell attachment, division, differentiation, proliferation, and serving as carriers for growth factors. Graphene is expected to render the entire surface area of the substratum appropriate for use as a biocompatible and flexible scaffold for osteogenic differentiation and cell differentiation for osteogenic differentiation. For instance, research has demonstrated that 3D graphene foams can enhance osteogenic development and preserve the functionality of human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs). Moreover, studies have shown that periodontal ligament stem cells (PDLSC) can be coaxed into becoming mature osteoblasts by means of increased mineralization rates and graphene-related upregulation of genes and proteins associated with bone, regardless of whether chemical inductors are additionally used.

Furthermore, Wang et al. investigated the utilization of an extrusion-based additive manufacturing system to fabricate poly(ε-caprolactone) (PCL)/pristine graphene scaffolds designed for bone tissue applications. The study also delved into the impact of chemical surface modification on the biological behavior of these scaffolds. The results indicate that surface modification enhances cell responsiveness, while adding pure graphene increases cell viability and proliferation [159]. To improve the mechanical strength of bioactive glass (BG), Fan et al. incorporated graphene nanosheets into BG to form a scaffold. Scaffolds reinforced with graphene nanosheets exhibited enhanced biocompatibility, increased cell proliferation, and a tenfold improvement in mechanical strength. Osteoblast cells attached and spread extensively on scaffolds with graphene nanosheets. The scaffolds coated with graphene nanosheets exhibited an enhanced ability to promote the growth of osteoblast cells and boost alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity, indicating their potential for promoting bone formation [160] . For bone tissue engineering, Cheng et al. mixed graphene with nano-58S bioactive glass using a selective laser sintering technique to manufacture the scaffolds. The study found that the use of graphene significantly enhanced the scaffold’s mechanical robustness. An HCA layer with a calcium/phosphorus ratio 1.69 was produced by immersing the scaffolds in a SBF solution for seven continuous days throughout the cell culture operation. Research using cell cultures confirmed that the graphene-containing scaffolds promoted the adhesion and growth of human MG63 cells. The use of graphene was associated with an increase in cell proliferation [161].

A cutting-edge technique known as cardiac tissue engineering (CTE) integrates biosynthetic implants, proangiogenic growth factors, differentiation factors, biological matrices, and cells with the ability to regenerate damaged heart tissue [162–164]. In CTE, the primary goals are the creation of cellular matrices, the verification of steady contractile activity, the establishment of electromechanical connection and functional vascularization, and the validation of these outcomes [165,166]. Graphene and its chemical compounds may enhance cell adhesion, proliferation, and differentiation of heart muscle cells because of their single-layer honeycomb lattice. Cardiovascular disorders (CD) and the prevention of blood clots on mechanical heart valves are the most common uses of graphene in CTE. To promote vasculogenesis and maintain the viability of transplanted cells, an ideal artificial heart scaffold in CTE would replicate these morphological, electrophysiological, and mechanical characteristics [167,168].

To elicit a therapeutic effect in animals or humans, a pharmaceutical ingredient needs to be administered through a process known as drug delivery. The drug delivery system encompasses technologies related to drug formulation, administration routes, targeting sites, metabolism, and toxicity [169]. Presently, the field of drug delivery is dedicated to enhancing drug resistance systems and developing targeted delivery systems, where the drug remains active only in specific areas of the body. When the issue of GO's solubility under physiological conditions is resolved, it will become a prime contender for drug delivery applications. Currently, extensive research coverage has been dedicated to graphene oxide (GO) nanocomposites, emphasizing their potential applications in drug delivery. Pi-pi stacking interactions significantly influence the substantial loading of anticancer drugs onto graphene oxide (GO). Its exceptionally wide surface area exposes every atom on both sides [170]. In 2008, Liu et al. became the first to use GO as a nanocarrier to deliver non-soluble chemoagents. This study formed NGO-PEG-SN38 by loading a soluble variant of NGO in physiological circumstances with SN38, an analog of camptothecin (CPT), through noncovalent van der Waals interactions. With an estimated 10% drug loading percentage, the water-soluble NGO-PEG-SN38 exhibited toxicity comparable to SN38 in DMSO and much greater potency than irinotecan (CPT-11) [171]. Concurrently, the NGO PEG/doxorubicin (DOX) drug-delivery system was initially reported by Sun et al., who worked on the task [172]. These two articles, which came out nearly simultaneously, are regarded as pioneering efforts in the field of GO drug delivery, which paved the way for numerous subsequent advancements. Most recently, Trusek et al. revealed graphene oxide as a potential drug carrier in a study. He showed an entirely innovative solution: the core–shell drug carrier proposed to be built on GO flakes and for which enzymatic hydrolysis is used to control the rate of DOX drug release. It is considered a long-term therapy approach due to enzymatic hydrolysis, which controls the release of doxorubicin (DOX) from the chemically activated graphene oxide (GO) nanocarrier [173].

When it comes to pharmaceutical agent delivery, GO has undoubtedly been the subject of more research than pure graphene. Zhang et al. presented a novel strategy in 2010 to use functionalized GO in biological research. Covalent folic acid binding was employed to target MCF-7 cells, a human breast cancer cell line expressing folic acid receptors. This was followed by sulfonic acid group functionalization, which made GO stable in physiological conditions. Compared to the material that was simply loaded with one of the anticancer medicines, this system that contained both DOX and camptothecin exhibited significantly higher cytotoxicity and showed precise targeting of MCF-7 cells [174]. Moreover, previous studies have demonstrated that GO can be utilized to monitor specifically targeted cancer, prevent tumor growth, and restrict the movement of tumor cells. In 2014, scientists utilized a near-infrared (NIR) laser to capture transdermal nanographene oxide-hyaluronic acid (NGO-HA) conjugates intending to treat melanoma, a type of skin cancer. However, research utilizing GO in CSC therapy for cancer treatment is limited. In the course of one of these studies, GO was investigated as a potential replacement that may potentially cross the blood–brain barrier in order to accomplish the goal of eliminating cancer cells through the action of DOX [175]. Zhang et al. demonstrated an innovative strategy that could improve the therapeutic effectiveness of cancer treatment by combining local targeted chemotherapy with external near-infrared (NIR) photothermal therapy utilizing a GO-PEG-DOX conjugate. This combination of treatments could be performed in order to treat cancer [176]. Additionally, GO was employed in order to simultaneously coat its surface with two or more drugs via this method [177,178].

The detection of a wide variety of biological moieties was accomplished by the invention of sensors based on graphene oxide (GO). In particular, the fluorescence quenching property of GO was a significant factor that contributed to the enhancement of the probe's sensitivity. Moreover, GO was utilized for specific DNA detection with minimal background signal. A nanocomposite consisting of GO and silver (Ag) ions was employed in bacterial detection. The synergistic action released Ag ions, contributing to the bactericidal effect. For this, an electrochemical approach was used to examine the efficiency of this sensor, and it was compared to the standard Dot blot assay [179]. In the pursuit of DNA detection, a novel nanocomposite was formulated by combining GO with gold (Au) nanorods (GO@Au nanorods). This sophisticated DNA sensor, built upon the nanocomposite, displayed exceptional selectivity, successfully discriminating complementary DNA sequences from a large pool of DNA, even in the presence of single-base mismatches at a ratio of 1000:1 [180].

Several investigations have indicated that nanocomposites based on GO can be utilized to detect adenosine triphosphate (ATP). One such proposed nanocomposite involved combining GO with a hairpin-shaped molecular aptamer beacon (MAB). Without ATP, this nanocomposite exhibited reduced fluorescence intensity [181]. Upon interaction with ATP, the nanocomposite experienced a resurgence in fluorescence intensity, displaying minimal background signal. Comparatively, covalently connected aptamer probes featuring amino dual modifications and fluorophore modifications exhibited enhanced ATP detection capabilities compared to physisorbed aptamer probes. The covalently coupled aptamer probes on GO provided superior fluorescent signals during the imaging of intracellular ATP [182].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene oxide in antibacterial and antiviral activity
ce:section.ce:para: Antibacterial capabilities are another area where GO-based composites show great promise as potential options. A small number of nanocomposites based on GO demonstrated superior antibacterial activity compared to the already available materials. The methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus strains were the target of the chitosan@AgNPs@GO nanocomposite, which proved highly effective antibacterial efficiency. AgNPs and GO@AgNP materials were less effective against bacteria than this nanocomposite, which demonstrated superior antibacterial activity [183]. In a study by Huang et al., a nanocomposite is prepared by polylactic acid (PLA) with graphene oxide (GO) and zinc oxide (ZnO) nanofillers, which showed improved mechanical strength and antibacterial activity, among other properties [184].

In addition to antibacterial activity, graphene oxide has also been found to possess antiviral properties, which can be linked to its unique nanosheet structure and negative charge [154]. An example of this work is demonstrated by Sametband et al., who, by mimicking the cell surface receptor heparan sulphate synthesized a graphene oxide derivative (rGO–SO3) and showed that this composite can prevent HSV-1 infection [185]. In another experiment conducted by Huang et al., multifunctional GO composites were developed, proving their ability to inhibit respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) infection by rapidly inactivating the virus and decreasing adhesion [186]. Recent research by Salama et al. demonstrated the antiviral activity of the hydrogel nanocomposite (TA@ZnO/GO/CS) incorporating chitosan NPs, ZnO NPs, tannic acid (TA) and GO sheets. The hydrogel nanocomposite represents a flexible framework for integrating antibacterial activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, as well as antifungal and anti-Herpes virus (HSV-1) applications acting as a “one stone, three birds” multifunctional approach. Notably, this hydrogel exhibited superior effectiveness against the HSV-1 virus, inhibiting it by approximately 86%. Moreover, hydrogels offer a novel approach to enhancing antiviral surfaces, personal protective equipment (PPE), and sprays due to their exceptional swelling properties. Furthermore, they are considered safe and responsive to changes in pH [187].

To create biomimetic tissue structures for organ transplantation and diagnostic and therapeutic research, tissue engineering is a multifaceted field that incorporates information in medicine, biology, and engineering [188]. Distinctive features of graphene and graphene oxide, such as remarkable mechanical strength and stiffness, improved electrical conductivity, and surface functionality, generated interest among researchers with great attention for bone and neutral tissue engineering [189]. It is common practice to use graphene-based nanomaterials on their own or in combination with other nanomaterials to include them into bone implants and scaffolds. This is done with the intention of enhancing the process of bone regeneration by increasing cellular adhesion, cellular proliferation, and osteoblastic differentiation. According to the findings of a study that Kim and colleagues carried out, the inclusion of graphene and its chemical derivatives into materials utilized for bone regeneration can enhance osteoconductivity. This is accomplished by stimulating both biomineralization and cellular osteogenic differentiation. Biomineralization might be improved, for instance, by combining biominerals like calcium carbonate (CaCO3) with graphene and GO sheets [190]. Furthermore, the three-dimensional scaffold created by Shin et al. was composed of GO-GelMA hydrogel and seeded with cells that showed superior cellular spreading and alignment and increased vitality and proliferation in an appropriate three-dimensional environment. Because GO is present, this 3D hydrogel scaffold has improved electrical characteristics and customizable mechanical strength.

Additionally, GO inclusion ensured the development of intricate artificial tissues with enhanced cellular performance and mechanical stability [189]. By supplying the proper microenvironment, this sort of 3D scaffolds could be modified to build various tissue constructs, including blood vessels, skin, skeletal muscle, and connective tissue. Despite the effectiveness of graphene-based 3D scaffolds in a variety of engineering contexts, including the heart, skin, bone, and neurons, they may soon prove to be a fruitful tool for hepatocyte culture for liver tissue engineering. More recently, Negin et al. assessed the biocompatibility of graphene sheets with primary adult cardiac cells (CMs). This work involved culturing adult rat ventricular myocytes on graphene-coated coverslips, revealing that graphene nanoparticles were beneficial for both cell types. Graphene substrates did not compromise cell viability and markedly improved cell attachment. The research additionally discovered that a synthetic multipart, particularly the macrophage-targeting/polarizing GO complex, could reduce the reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced by macrophages. A combination of macrophage complex and IL-4 pDNA was utilized to facilitate the differentiation of M1 to M2 macrophages and the release of regenerative cytokines for cardiac purposes, thereby diminishing inflammation and enhancing heart function in animal models, as illustrated in Fig. 12
[191] .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in biomedical Sciences

ce:section.ce:para: Numerous nanocarriers have been designed to enhance the solubility of pharmaceuticals in water and to target them precisely. Graphene quantum dots have distinct characteristics that render them highly suitable for encapsulating therapeutic substances and enhancing stability in physiological environments. These characteristics encompass a surface abundant in oxygen and a single layer of atoms with extremely small lateral dimensions. Typically, multifunctional GQDs perform the dual roles of targeted cellular imaging and drug carrier concurrently. Because of the intrinsic fluorescence of GQDs, it is possible to visualize drug delivery systems by utilizing organic fluorophores and semiconductor QDs to comprehend cellular uptake.

Additionally, we can readily observe cellular mobility in actual time without using external dyes. In a study conducted by Wang et al., the potential to load the anticancer medication doxorubicin (DOX) onto synthetic folic acid (FA)-conjugated GQDs was investigated. The created nanoassembly could effectively deliver the medication to the target cells and clearly differentiate between cancerous and normal cells. As a result, the intrinsic constant fluorescence of GQDs allows real-time tracking of the drug release process and the absorption of the DOX–GQD–FA nanoassembly by cells [192]. The Zhu group efficiently developed a method to transport DOX using a zeolite imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) containing GQDs. The study showed that an acidic pH influenced the drug release characteristics of ZIF-8/GQD nanoparticles containing DOX. Considering the development of DOX-loaded GQD and linking with Cy5.5 dye via a cathepsin D-responsive peptide, the Wei group took advantage of GQDs' notable physicochemical features [193]. The drug-loaded nanoconjugate demonstrated enhanced cellular uptake and tissue penetration, leading to better therapeutic efficacy in laboratory and living organisms. Using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM), the 4T1 cell uptake and DOX release were demonstrated. Moreover, in a study conducted by Wang et al., it was shown that a ligand-functionalized DOX-loaded GQD-folic acid nanocarrier enhanced real-time monitoring of cellular absorption, targeted drug administration, and selective cell labeling [194].

Biosensing is another hotspot that can benefit from the optical characteristics of GQDs alongside bioimaging. The primary function of GQDs in biosensing is to identify and indicate the existence of biomolecules [203]. As mentioned earlier, the optical properties of GQDs can be changed by modifying the electron structure of the edge groups [204,205]. GQD-based biosensors exploit the attraction between particular functional groups within GQDs to detect biomolecules that include analytes. As a result of the binding of a functional group conjugated to the GQD to the analyte, various electronic states can be produced by the pair. Moreover, an analyte can be detected as a change in PL intensity by modifying the electrical structure of the GQD [206]. It has been demonstrated that GQD-based biosensor systems can detect ions, DNA, and various other compounds. The photostability of GQDs and the rapid reaction of PL-based systems position GQDs as a suitable platform for biosensing, which demands excellent selectivity, sensitivity, and simplicity. GQDs, due to their high surface-to-volume ratio, excellent biocompatibility, plentiful hydrophilic edges, and partially hydrophobic plane promote protein adsorption, have been employed as electrode modifiers in the development of electrochemical enzymatic biosensors [206]. Not only does the modification of the electrode with GQDs maintain the functionality of the immobilized enzyme, but it also improves the electrochemical signal.

Furthermore, the nanoscale dimension of GQDs makes it possible to wire enzyme redox centers and, consequently, DET, particularly for oxidoreductases such as laccase. In an investigation, Muthurasu et al. detailed a biosensor for horseradish peroxidase (HRP) that used amidic interactions between the carboxylic groups of GQDs and the amine groups of the enzyme to permanently immobilize the enzyme onto a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) that had been modified with GQDs·H2O2 was detected with a LOD of approximately 530 nM using amperometric monitoring [207]. A biosensor was created to monitor H2O2 using cyclic voltammetry (CV). The biosensor utilised direct electron transfer (DET) of haemoglobin (Hb) that was immobilized on a nanocomposite of GQDs-chitosan (GQDs-Chit) [208]. For the first time, Vasilescu et al., proposed an electrochemical laccase biosensor for determining the polyphenol index in red wine. This biosensor used screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCE) modified with molybdenum sulphide (MoS2) nanoflakes and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Following graphene in terms of 2D layered material research, MoS2 has attracted much attention, and exfoliated MoS2 flakes have shown promising sensing capabilities. Unlike, the zero-gap semiconductor graphene, which does not contain MoS2, an electrocatalytic activity is caused by the existence of the bandgap for MoS2 (1.8 eV for a MoS2 monolayer). This finding illustrated that synergistic interaction between the GQD and MoS2 sheets resulted in an enhanced electrocatalytic activity and conductivity in the resulting enzyme biosensor, SPCE-MoS2-GQDs, as compared to SPCE-MoS2 and SPCE-GQDs, respectively [209].

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs in antibacterial and antiviral activity
