ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are thought to cause oxidative stress by producing reactive oxygen species (ROS) when exposed to light at 470 nm and 1 W [51]. In order to suppress the growth of bacteria that are resistant to methicillin, such as E. coli and Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), damage to the cell membrane is caused. In the same investigation, it was discovered that mouse spleen cells were less susceptible, which led researchers to conclude that GQDs have a selective antibacterial photodynamic effect [210]. In another study, it was observed that C60-graphene quantum dots (C60-GQDs) demonstrated the ability to eliminate S. aureus, including strains resistant to antibiotics. The initial step in disrupting cell envelopes involves the assembly of GQDs with bacterial cell surfaces, and this process is greatly influenced by the surface Gaussian-curvature match between the two [211,212]. Another study by Moradlou and colleagues showed the antimicrobial characteristics of a titanium (Ti) surface incorporating hematite. They observed that the modified surface demonstrated prolonged antibacterial effects against both S. mutans and E. coli. Consequently, they proposed that the combination of carbon quantum dots (CQD) with other nanomaterials, such as α-Fe2O3 (hematite), has the potential to effectively eliminate dental pathogens, encompassing both gram-negative and gram-positive strains [213]. Moreover, Teymourinia et al. proposed a nanocomposites as antibacterial pad prepared by employing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a linker, stabilizer, and reduction agent for the in-situ production of Ag nanoparticles (Ag NPs) on cotton pad. The results indicate that cotton/GQDs/Ag pad shows a very promising minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 0.09 and 0.01 against gram-positive and gram-negative S. aureus and E. coli, respectively. He also added the information that using GQDs as a linker (cotton/GQDs/Ag) and as a stabilizing agent (cotton/Ag/GQDs) significantly boosts the antibacterial activity of Ag NPs [214]. Another study by Rashki et al. found that a P. muralis/ZnO/GQDs-NCs formulation might be used as a new antibacterial agent to stop bacteria from developing and forming biofilms [215].

In addition to antibacterial activity, Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a new kind of antiviral nanomaterials that are cheap and have interesting physicochemical characteristics [216]. Due to their significant solubility (in aqueous solutions), environmental friendliness, robust chemical inertness, rapid modification capacity, non-toxicity, and outstanding biocompatibility, GQDs have garnered a lot of interest compared to other metal-oxide quantum dots [217]. Chemical functionalization of GQDs is possible by applying different surface functionalities on the basal plane or edge sites. This is because multivalent interactions facilitate virus attachment to host cells. Indeed, GQDs can attach a variety of ligands due to their intrinsically high surface-to-volume ratio and multivalent properties. Thus, functionalized GQDs may make it more difficult for viruses to enter cells. Recent ground-breaking research has shown that heteroatom doping can activate the antiviral characteristics of GQDs [218]. Combining surface-functionalized triazole derivatives with GQDs can produce powerful antiviral candidates that interact with cell membrane proteins or activate signal transduction pathways. More recently, Hsieh et al. reported developing and synthesizing N-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as an antiviral nanomaterial. These GQDs exhibited ultra-high inhibition (>99.9%) against Feline Coronavirus NTU156 (FCoV NTU156) and Enterovirus 71 (EV71) [219]. Next, Pijeira et al. conducted a different investigation in which they synthesized, characterized, and in vitro tested GQD-FA against ZIKV infection. The results of this study demonstrated that GQD-FA possesses a powerful antiviral impact against the replication of ZIKV infection [220]. The findings of this study unequivocally demonstrate that GQDs possess effective antiviral action.

Tissue engineering, which uses cell interactions with growth factors and extracellular matrix (ECM) to create new biological materials, has recently emerged as a potential method for regenerating injured tissue and facilitating wound healing [221,222]. Eliminating surgical and postoperative care complications, tissue engineering may build scaffolds that are porous matrices for tissue regeneration. These scaffolds encourage the proliferation of cells and tissue restoration. In living organisms, these scaffolds serve as matrix outside cells, facilitating cellular adhesion and migration and transporting nutrients. Furthermore, they can modify cellular phase behavior through targeted mechanical and biological mechanisms. Adding GQDs nanoparticles to the scaffold helps enhance the shape memory properties of the nanocomposites. So, Nejad et al. have prepared polyurethane (PU) and GQDs nanocomposites along with nanofibers serving as scaffolds. These nanofibres contain varied concentrations of GQDs, such as 0.0, 0.5, and 1 wt%, which can be explored in tissue engineering applications. Moreover, this study aimed to investigate the impact of GQD concentration on the shape memory properties, morphology, porosity, cytotoxicity, biocompatibility, and cell viability of the resulting nanofibers.

The ultimate goal was to explore potential applications of the electrospun nanofibers in medical devices or as scaffolds in tissue engineering [223]. More recently, stem cell-based tissue engineering and reconstructive medicinal applications have evaluated bioactive GQDs. Qiu et al. investigated the significant function of bioactive GQDs during bone development. Bioactive GQDs were discovered to promote ontogenesis by activating its principal activation pathway [224]. Tissue engineers use bioactive GQDs as reinforcing agents to enhance the mechanical properties of scaffolds that can hold orthopedic implants. Bone, nerve, and cartilage benefit from bioactive GQDs [225,226]. Table 2 provides a concise overview of the research conducted on a diverse range of carbon-based nanomaterials for their application in the healthcare sector.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity and biocompatibility of carbon-based nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: The safety, toxicity, and biocompatibility of carbon-based nanomaterials (CBNs) are essential factors for their extensive utilization in healthcare and medical fields. Despite the exceptional features of CBNs for diverse biomedical applications, including drug delivery, bioimaging, biosensing, cancer therapy, and tissue engineering, apprehensions about their possible detrimental effects on human health have emerged. Studies have shown that the toxicity of CBNs can vary depending on factors such as size, purity, functionalization on the surface, and exposure concentration and route [234]. Many studies typically assess the toxicity of fullerenes in their native form; however, a method known as functionalization can improve their sustainability. Native fullerenes have considerable lipophilicity and can produce reactive oxygen species (ROS), resulting in oxidative damage. The functionalization of fullerenes with hydrophilic groups increases their solubility and reduces the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), hence enhancing their biocompatibility. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) toxicity is contingent upon their size, surface chemistry, and the presence of undesirable impurities. Researchers have discovered cytotoxicity, inflammation, and oxidative stress effects induced by natural CNTs. Utilizing the aforementioned carbon nanotubes in their unaltered state produces detrimental consequences on the biological environment [235].

Graphene and Graphene Oxide (GO) exhibit unique biological profiles influenced by their structural and chemical properties. Graphene is frequently linked to pro-inflammatory reactions, but graphene oxide (GO) generally displays anti-inflammatory characteristics. Various criteria, such as size, dose, and degree of functionalization, determine the divergent effects. Graphene oxide (GO), owing to its oxygen-rich functional groups, is intrinsically more hydrophilic than graphene, enhancing its dispersibility in aqueous environments and overall biocompatibility for biological and therapeutic uses. The increased hydrophilicity enables GO to interact more effectively with biological systems than the comparatively inert and hydrophobic graphene [236]. Numerous scientific studies show nanodiamonds are also biocompatible and of low toxicity. They are highly in demand for biological uses because of their favorable appearance, fluorescence, target cell selectivity, and nanometer size. Their surface can be easily modified with various biological molecules to improve their functionality and suitability for therapeutic, diagnostic, and other biological applications. Two important aspects that define their biocompatibility are surface chemistry and particle size distribution. This means their proper control is necessary for their safe and effective use in biological systems [237].

To a great extent, the biocompatibility of CBNs can also be enhanced through various surface modifications to decrease their inherent toxicity and improve their coupling with biological systems. Thus, it can be concluded that recognizing the toxicity and biocompatibility of CBNs is the key to the successful application of these materials in various biomedical fields. However, in vitro and in vivo studies are necessary to understand their biocompatibility and safety profiles for clinical use.

The study of carbon nanomaterials is at the intersection of multiple fields, including physics, chemistry, biology, and medicine. With the advent of these nanomaterials, new possibilities for investigating and altering biological processes have emerged. Collaborative efforts across disciplines are necessary to attain a perfect formulation. Therefore, we have an optimistic prospect for the upcoming development of carbon nanomaterials. This review aimed to highlight the place of carbon-based nanomaterials such as fullerenes, nanodiamonds, carbon nanotubes, graphene, and its derivatives, as well as graphene quantum dots for preclinic biomedical applications such as drug delivery, cancer therapy, biosensing antibacterial, antiviral and tissue engineering. Their exceptional optical and electrical characteristics, large surface area, acceptable biological compatibility, chemical stability, tunable surface chemistry, and high mechanical strength distinguish them from other contenders, and these features might be helpful in a wide range of biomedical contexts. In light of the pressing need to find a solution for incurable diseases and the recent surge in global health concerns, the development of CBN as carbon nanoarchitectonics has been expedited, particularly for diagnostic and therapeutic applications.

Notably, CBNs are emerging as potentially effective materials because of their organic π–π stacking characteristics and inorganic semiconducting properties. As a result, it can simultaneously interact with biomolecules and respond to light. These nanoscale structures can penetrate a range of biological membranes and structures to facilitate the distribution of pharmaceuticals. Furthermore, several chemical modification techniques have been designed and fabricated to improve the biocompatibility and effectiveness of CBNs while minimizing toxicity concerns. However, it should be highlighted that considerable thorough toxicological research is necessary to ascertain the toxicological effects and pharmacokinetics of CBNs.

Additionally, these carbon-based nanomaterials have the potential to be used in biomedicine in the future by combining these properties in a single unit. Therefore, CBN is anticipated to be a nanoplatform with great potential for clinical applications in the near future, with more preclinical studies. It has the capacity to be utilized for tissue repair, regenerative medicine, bioimaging, and other challenging disorders. Moreover, precise engineering of CBNs is essential to reduce their potential toxicity, enhance scalability, and ensure cost-effectiveness while retaining their beneficial properties. This careful optimization not only assures safety but also broadens their scope of application, enabling their use in diverse areas such as environmental remediation, energy storage, and conversion, renewable energy systems, catalytic processes, textile innovation, agricultural advancements, flexible electronics, aerospace, defense, many other emerging industries, where they can offer groundbreaking innovations and improvements in performance, efficiency, and sustainability.

Munna Dan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Pawan Singh: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Roshan Mathew: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Manisha Agrawal: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation.
